topics.title: Semantic Drift in Italian NPN Construction: A Comparative Study of Contextual Embeddings vs. Explicit Linguistic Theories
topics.rationale: Recent work on Italian NPN construction in BERT (Layer su Layer) identifies a critical gap regarding the disambiguation of contextual embeddings against explicit linguistic theories. While existing studies focus on layer-wise probing, they lack a rigorous evaluation of how these embeddings align with explicit grammatical rules in the Italian NPN family. This research addresses the specific limitation of previous experimental designs by testing contextual representations against explicit linguistic constraints. Furthermore, it investigates whether the structural ambiguity in Italian NPN is better resolved by embedding proximity or by adherence to explicit syntactic hierarchies, filling a significant gap in interpretability research for specific morphological constructions.
topics.approach: We will employ a multi-stage methodology combining layer-wise probing classifiers trained on contextual vector representations with a formal evaluation of explicit linguistic theories. The study will analyze the semantic drift of Italian NPN forms across different layers of the BERT model, contrasting these findings with the predictions of explicit grammatical rules. We will use statistical tests to determine if contextual embeddings alone can resolve ambiguities that explicit theories can, or if the latter provides a necessary grounding for interpretation.

topics.title: Reliability Assessment of Multi-Teacher Distillation in Low-Resource Summarization: An Analysis of Data Scaling vs. Supervision
topics.rationale: Existing literature on multi-teacher knowledge distillation for low-resource summarization lacks a reliability-aware perspective, specifically failing to distinguish between scenarios where data scaling outperforms loss engineering versus when teacher supervision is more effective. This gap is critical as current models often rely on data scaling without verifying the reliability of the distilled knowledge. By introducing an entropy-weighted agreement-aware distillation framework, this research aims to provide a robust metric for evaluating the quality of knowledge transfer in low-resource settings. The study will quantify how data scaling impacts reliability gains compared to the benefits of multi-teacher supervision, offering a nuanced view of model training dynamics.
topics.approach: We propose a novel evaluation framework that integrates Entropy Weighted Agreement Aware Distillation (EWAD) with Cross-lingual pseudo label Knowledge Distillation to measure the reliability of multi-teacher supervision. The approach involves analyzing the convergence of token-level mechanisms and geometric constraints across different training stages to determine the optimal balance between data scaling and supervision. We will validate these findings using human-validated multi-judge LLM evaluation metrics to ensure the reliability gains are not merely statistical artifacts.

topics.title: The Syntactic-World Knowledge Integration Gap: A Comparative Analysis of LLMs and Human Ambiguity Resolution in Turkish
topics.rationale: While LLMs demonstrate strong performance on many language tasks, it remains unclear whether they integrate world knowledge with syntactic structure in a human-like, structure-sensitive manner during ambiguity resolution. Existing research has not clearly demonstrated whether LLMs achieve plausibility effects similar to humans when resolving Turkish prenominal relative-clause ambiguities. This study aims to bridge the gap between model performance and human cognition by directly comparing the mechanisms used by LLMs against human judgment in a controlled setting. Understanding this integration is crucial for developing more robust and interpretable models in domains requiring complex semantic reasoning.
topics.approach: We will conduct a parallel preference-based setup where human annotators and LLMs are presented with Turkish prenominal relative-clause ambiguities. The study will analyze the mean per-token log-probability of the models to measure their confidence and the plausibility effect of human ratings. By comparing the speeded forced-choice comprehension results, we will isolate the specific factors contributing to human success versus model failure in this specific syntactic ambiguity resolution task.

topics.title: Linguistically Motivated Quality Metrics for Diglossic Languages: Diagnosing Errors in Arabic Dialects Beyond Surface Form
topics.rationale: Existing MT evaluation frameworks are largely language-agnostic and fail to capture dialect-specific errors in diglossic languages like Arabic, where translation failures stem from mismatches in language variety, content coverage, and pragmatic appropriateness rather than surface form alone. Current metrics rely on standard spBLEU scores, which do not diagnose errors through linguistically grounded levels that account for sociolinguistics, pragmatics, and semantics. This research addresses the critical need for a framework that can diagnose MT errors through six specific linguistic domains, providing a more accurate assessment of translation quality in culturally rich contexts.
topics.approach: We will develop the Linguistically Motivated Multidimensional Quality Metrics (LQM) framework by constructing a hierarchical error taxonomy that includes six linguistically grounded error levels: sociolinguistics, pragmatics, semantics, morphosyntax, orthography, and graphetics. The approach will involve zero-shot evaluation of six different LLMs on a curated corpus of seven Arabic dialects, followed by expert span-level human annotation to validate the severity-weighted quality scores. This method will allow for a comparative analysis of how different model architectures handle errors across these varied linguistic domains.
datasets: Seven Arabic dialects: Egyptian, Emirati, Jordanian, Mauritanian, Moroccan, Palestinian, and Yemeni

topics.title: Typos and ESL Variation as Co-occurring Factors in LLM Performance: A Trans-EnV Framework Analysis
topics.rationale: Large Language Models trained on English data perform best on English inputs, yet non-native English speakers (ESL) often interact with them in English, encountering typographical errors. Existing research has studied the effects of ESL variation and typographical errors separately, ignoring their co-occurrence in real-world use, which likely leads to an overestimation of model performance in realistic settings. This research addresses the gap in evaluating LLM behavior in realistic settings by analyzing the combined effects of ESL variation and typos, moving beyond clean standard English evaluations. Understanding these co-occurring factors is essential for improving the robustness of LLMs in diverse, real-world user interactions.
topics.approach: We will utilize the Trans-EnV framework and MulTypo analysis to systematically evaluate the impact of Low-level, Moderate-level, and Severe-level typos alongside various ESL variants on LLM performance. The study will employ closed-ended and open-ended tasks to measure how the presence of typos and non-native linguistic features alters the model's output distribution. By isolating and quantifying the specific contributions of each factor, we will provide a comprehensive view of model sensitivity to real-world linguistic noise and variation.

topics.title: Temporal Memory Decay in Mamba Models: Quantifying the Shadow Model Bottleneck using Per-Token Distributional Statistics
topics.rationale: While recent works like 'Learning the Signature of Memorization' utilize unlimited labeled data to bypass the shadow model bottleneck, they lack a rigorous temporal framework to quantify how quickly this signature decays over long sequences. This gap is critical for fine-tuning autoregressive models where long-term context retention is essential. Existing methods often treat memorization as a static property, ignoring the dynamic nature of attention mechanisms in Mamba and RWKV architectures. Understanding the precise temporal window during which a model retains its memorized patterns is necessary to optimize training schedules and evaluate the effectiveness of state-space models in long-horizon tasks.
topics.approach: This research will employ a novel per-token distributional statistics analysis pipeline applied to Mamba and RWKV-4 architectures. We will construct a synthetic temporal decay curve by extracting sequence embeddings at varying time steps relative to the end of the generation window. By correlating these statistical signatures with the model's gradient updates during cross-entropy loss minimization, we will isolate the specific temporal window where memorization is most robust.

topics.title: Domain-Specific Legal Knowledge Retrieval in Turkish Law: Evaluating the Impact of Token Span Masking on Domain Adaptation
topics.rationale: Despite the availability of general-purpose models like LEGAL-BERT for English, the field of Turkish LegalTech suffers from a severe scarcity of high-volume, domain-specific data. Existing studies often fail to leverage the full potential of domain-adaptive pre-training methodologies, such as targeted keyword masking, due to a lack of curated Turkish legal corpora. This study aims to bridge that gap by systematically evaluating how specific masking strategies, including Word Span Masking, influence the retrieval accuracy of Turkish legal documents compared to standard masking techniques.
topics.approach: We will conduct a comparative experiment using the 18 GB cleaned legal corpus to test the efficacy of Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training (DAPT) combined with various masking variants. The methodology will involve a controlled ablation study where we measure the Top-1 accuracy and document pass rate across different token masking configurations to determine the optimal strategy for Turkish legal retrieval.

topics.title: Disambiguating Italian NPN Constructions in BERT: A Layer-wise Probing Classifier Approach
topics.rationale: Current interpretability research often focuses on English or general-purpose models, leaving the Italian NPN (noun-preposition-noun) constructional family underexplored. While BERT effectively encodes contextual information, the specific linguistic theories governing Italian NPN structures remain ambiguous within the model's internal representations. This research seeks to resolve these ambiguities by applying explicit linguistic theories to layer-wise probing classifiers, thereby mapping out the precise linguistic information encoded in the BERT family for this lesser-investigated language.
topics.approach: The study will utilize a layer-wise probing classifier architecture trained on Italian NPN data to disentangle the contributions of different transformer layers to the encoding of noun-preposition-noun constructions. By comparing contextual vector representations against explicit linguistic theories, we will identify which layers encode the most critical syntactic features of Italian NPN structures.

topics.title: Cross-Layer Surprisal Calibration: Quantifying the Gap Between Early Layer Surprisal and Human Cognitive Effort on Challenging Constructions
topics.rationale: Existing work demonstrates that early layers of LLMs effectively model human sentence processing on unchallenging constructions, yet they underestimate cognitive effort on syntactically challenging ones. This gap suggests that current models fail to capture the full cognitive load required for complex syntactic structures, leading to a significant discrepancy between predicted and actual processing time. Addressing this is crucial for developing more robust models that can accurately simulate human reasoning in complex linguistic contexts.
topics.approach: This research will employ a dual-alignment framework to compare the surprisal metrics generated by different layers of large language models against empirical human reading times on challenging English constructions. We will analyze the probability-update measures across shallow and deep layers to pinpoint exactly where the model's internal representation diverges from human cognitive processing demands.

topics.title: Multilingual Language Steering via Sparse Autoencoders: Controlling LLM Outputs with Monolingual Data Constraints
topics.rationale: While LLMs exhibit strong multilingual capabilities, reliably controlling their language output remains a significant challenge, particularly when relying on expensive multilingual or parallel data. Existing Sparse Autoencoder (SAE)-based approaches often face the same data constraint, limiting their applicability in low-resource scenarios. This study proposes a novel method to discover sparse language-specific features using monolingual data, offering a scalable solution for language steering without requiring parallel corpora.
topics.approach: We will implement a Sparse Autoencoder framework to identify sparse language-specific features from monolingual data, utilizing Random-token Filtering and Directional ablation techniques. By leveraging Cross-entropy loss and steering vectors, we will demonstrate the ability to generate controlled language outputs, thereby mitigating the data scarcity bottleneck in multilingual generation.

articles.title: Benchmarking Multilingual Speech Models on Pashto: Zero-Shot ASR, Script Failure, and Cross-Domain Evaluation

articles_with_facts.abstract: Recent advances in natural language processing (NLP) have increasingly enabled LegalTech applications, yet existing studies specific to Turkish law have still been limited due to the scarcity of domain-specific data and models. articles_with_facts.title: 'Layer su Layer': Identifying and Disambiguating the Italian NPN Construction in BERT's family
articles_with_facts.abstract: Interpretability research has highlighted the importance of evaluating Pretrained Language Models (PLMs) and in particular contextual embeddings against explicit linguistic theories to determine what linguistic information they encode. This study focuses on the Italian NPN (noun-preposition-noun) constructional family, challenging some of the theoretical and methodological assumptions underlying previous experimental designs and extending this type of research to a lesser-investigated language.

articles_with_facts.abstract: Latent reasoning models (LRMs) have attracted significant research interest due to their low inference cost (relative to explicit reasoning models) and theoretical ability to explore multiple reasoning paths in parallel. articles_with_facts.abstract: A recent study (Kuribayashi et al., 2025) has shown that human sentence processing behavior, typically measured on syntactically unchallenging constructions, can be effectively modeled using surprisal from early layers of large language models (LLMs). articles_with_facts.title: Unlocking Prompt Infilling Capability for Diffusion Language Models
articles_with_facts.abstract: Masked diffusion language models (dLMs) generate text through bidirectional denoising, yet this capability remains locked for infilling prompts. articles_with_facts.abstract: We present a method to identify a valence-arousal (VA) subspace within large language model representations. topics.title: Transferable Learned Membership Inference Attacks for Fine-tuned Autoregressive Models Using RWKV-4
topics.rationale: Existing membership inference attacks for fine-tuned language models rely on hand-crafted heuristics that are bounded by designer intuition. This paper demonstrates that fine-tuning yields unlimited labeled data, enabling transferable learned attacks that bypass the shadow model bottleneck. By leveraging sequence classification over per-token distributional statistics and gradient descent on cross-entropy loss, we can construct effective attacks without requiring explicit labeled membership data.
topics.approach: We propose a transferable learned attack framework utilizing RWKV-4 attention mechanisms and Mamba state-space models. The method involves training a sequence classifier on per-token distributional statistics derived from the fine-tuned model's input-output pairs, followed by gradient descent optimization on the cross-entropy loss to maximize membership inference accuracy.

topics.title: Domain-Specific Legal NLP for Turkish Law: A High-Volume Corpus Analysis for LEGAL-BERT
topics.rationale: Recent advances in Natural Language Processing have enabled LegalTech applications, yet existing studies specific to Turkish law remain scarce. While LEGAL-BERT is developed for English legal texts, there is a critical lack of a domain-specific high-volume counterpart for the Turkish legal domain. This research addresses the scarcity of domain-specific data by analyzing the Turkish legal corpus.
topics.approach: We apply Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training (DAPT) methodology using a 18 GB cleaned legal corpus. The approach involves implementing Whole-Word Masking, Token Span Masking, and targeted Keyword Masking, utilizing a 48K WordPiece tokenizer to ensure robustness against unseen legal terminology.

topics.title: The Impact of Prompt Injection on Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey
topics.rationale: Prompt injection attacks are a significant security concern for Large Language Models (LLMs). However, current literature often lacks a comprehensive survey that systematically evaluates the effectiveness and impact of these attacks across different model architectures and deployment scenarios. This survey aims to fill this gap.
topics.approach: We conduct a comprehensive survey of prompt injection attacks, evaluating their effectiveness and impact across different model architectures and deployment scenarios. The survey covers various types of prompts, including malicious code, SQL injection, and social engineering, and analyzes the resulting vulnerabilities in LLMs.

articles.abstract: Determining whether two graphs are isomorphic is a fundamental problem with practical applications in areas such as molecular chemistry or social network analysis, yet it remains a challenging task, with exact solutions often being computationally expensive. We address this task using persistent homology built on motif-based filtrations of graphs, a method from topological data analysis that summarizes the shape of data by tracking the persistence of structural features along filtrations. articles.title: A Null Model for Mapper Subtype Claims
articles.abstract: The Mapper algorithm from topological data analysis constructs a graph summarizing the shape of a high-dimensional dataset, and groups of data points identified within this graph are widely interpreted as evidence of distinct subtypes. However, the covariance structure of the data alone can make such groups appear differentiated, even when no subtypes are present. Existing validation approaches do not account for this effect and thus cannot distinguish covariance artifacts from genuine subtypes.

topics.title: Cross-Domain Graph Isomorphism via Persistent Homology Motif Filtering with Null Model Correction
topics.rationale: Existing graph isomorphism methods struggle with high-dimensional data where symmetries and covariance structures lead to false positives. The provided paper [1] introduces motif-based filtrations but lacks a robust null model to distinguish true topological signals from data artifacts. Paper [2] offers a critical gap in validating subtype claims using a Gaussian null model against Mapper artifacts, which is essential for cross-domain applications. By combining these, we can develop a framework that filters persistent homology motifs to ensure isomorphism detection is not confounded by the covariance geometry inherent in real-world datasets.
topics.approach: We will adapt the motif-based filtration framework from [1] to detect structural equivalence in high-dimensional graphs. We will incorporate the Gaussian null model proposed in [2] to construct a test statistic that measures mean-level differentiation between Mapper communities, effectively isolating genuine subtypes from covariance artifacts. This approach will be validated using synthetic graphs with known symmetries and real-world biological networks to assess accuracy and computational cost.

topics.title: Validating Mapper Subtype Claims in High-Dimensional Data Using Covariance Geometry Correction
topics.rationale: Recent studies on topological data analysis have highlighted the difficulty of distinguishing genuine subtypes from artifacts caused by the covariance structure of high-dimensional data. Paper [2] explicitly identifies the lack of validation approaches that account for covariance geometry, creating a significant gap in reliable subtype classification. We propose a method that leverages the Mapper algorithm's output but applies a covariance-aware null model to verify community membership, ensuring that detected subtypes reflect true data structure rather than statistical artifacts.
topics.approach: The research will focus on developing a validation pipeline for Mapper-based subtype detection that explicitly models the covariance geometry of the input data. We will employ the test statistic measuring mean-level differentiation between communities, as detailed in [2], to create a rigorous null distribution. This will allow us to distinguish genuine subtypes from those generated solely by the covariance structure of the data, addressing the gap identified in [2].

topics.title: Topological Sensitivity Analysis in Connectome-Constrained Neural Networks Using Drosophila Connectome Data
topics.rationale: While connectome-constrained neural networks show early performance advantages over random controls, interpreting these gains as evidence of biological graph topology is confounded by initialization and null-model differences. Paper [3] provides a critical methodological gap by offering a detailed evaluation framework that includes weak controls, stricter controls, and five-sample degree-preserving ensembles. Integrating these rigorous controls with the flyvis-based study approach will allow for a clearer distinction between genuine biological topological benefits and initialization artifacts.
topics.approach: We will implement a comparative study using the Drosophila connectome dataset and the specific evaluation metrics outlined in [3], including early loss, mean activity, and runtime. The approach will involve constructing naive self-loop-matched random graphs and degree-preserving rewired null models to establish a baseline. By analyzing the activity scales and runtime differences across these controlled conditions, we can isolate the specific contribution of biological graph topology to neural learning efficiency.

topics.title: Learning on Graphs via Contraction and Hourglass Persistence: Addressing Expressivity and Stability Limits
topics.rationale: The integration of persistent homology into Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) has expanded expressivity but introduced limitations regarding learnability and stability. Paper [4] identifies that existing PH methods rely on traversing an increasing sequence of subgraphs, which this paper argues has shortcomings. We propose a hybrid approach combining Contraction Homology and Hourglass Persistence to overcome these sequence-based limitations, ensuring more stable and expressive learning pipelines in GNNs.
topics.approach: This research will focus on developing an end-to-end differentiable GNN pipeline that utilizes Contraction Homology and Hourglass Persistence to address the expressivity and stability issues identified in [4]. We will evaluate the method on standard real-world graph datasets to measure improvements in learnability and stability compared to traditional PH methods. The approach will specifically target the limitations of increasing subgraph sequences by utilizing contraction sequences and simplicial networks.

topics.title: Topology of Kostlan Random Real Algebraic Curves: Expected Components and Nests via Barrier Method
topics.rationale: The topology of Kostlan random real algebraic plane curves remains a challenging area, particularly regarding the expected number of connected components and nests. Existing results often lack rigorous bounds for specific growth conditions, creating a gap in theoretical understanding. We aim to bridge this by developing a novel barrier method variant to derive lower bounds and analyze the probability of points remaining in different components, providing a more complete theoretical framework.
topics.approach: The study will employ the barrier method to address questions about the topology of Kostlan random real algebraic plane curves. We will derive rigorous lower bounds for the expected number of connected components and nests, addressing the limitations noted in [5]. This involves analyzing subspaces and probability distributions to understand the growth conditions of curve complexity. The results will provide a foundation for understanding the limits of topological bounds in algebraic geometry.

topics.title: Evaluating Mapper Subtype Claims Using Null Models and Covariance Geometry
topics.rationale: Recent studies using Mapper algorithms have identified significant subtypes in high-dimensional data, yet the validity of these findings is often questioned by the underlying covariance structure of the data. Current validation approaches fail to account for how the covariance geometry of the input data influences the Mapper's community detection, leading to potential false positives. This research addresses the gap by developing a rigorous statistical framework to distinguish genuine biological or social subtypes from artifacts induced by the algorithm's reliance on covariance geometry.
topics.approach: We propose a null model framework that explicitly incorporates the covariance geometry of the input data into the Mapper algorithm's validation process. By comparing the mean-level differentiation between Mapper communities against a distribution generated by permuting labels while preserving the original covariance structure, we can isolate the effect of true subtypes from data-induced artifacts. We will implement this using the Mapper algorithm on breast cancer gene expression and lower-grade glioma genomics datasets, applying a permutation baseline test statistic to measure Type I error rates.

topics.title: Designing Persistent Homology for 3D Point Clouds: A Unified Architecture for Deep Learning
topics.rationale: While Persistent Homology (PH) is successfully applied to graph data and molecular structures, its integration into 3D point cloud deep learning remains ad hoc and architecturally peripheral. Existing methods lack a unified design space that formalizes the interplay between complex filtration strategies, persistence representations, and neural backbones. This research aims to bridge this gap by constructing a comprehensive design space that standardizes the construction, filtration, and representation of PH descriptors within 3D point cloud processing pipelines.
topics.approach: We propose a unified design space for PH in 3D point clouds that integrates diagram computation, vectorization, and neural backbones into a cohesive architecture. The approach will involve formalizing the relationship between the choice of filtration strategy (e.g., Vietoris-Rips vs. LSH) and the resulting persistence representation, alongside optimizing neural backbones for topology-sensitive discrimination. We will evaluate this design space using the ModelNet40 and ShapeNetPart datasets, focusing on robustness to noise, computational scalability, and the ability to capture part consistency.

topics.title: Topological Sensitivity in Connectome-Constrained Neural Networks
topics.rationale: Connectome-constrained neural networks are frequently evaluated against sparse random controls to claim that biological graph topology improves learning efficiency. However, existing evaluations often interpret performance advantages without adequately controlling for initialization and null-model confounds, potentially masking spurious correlations. This research seeks to critically evaluate these claims by establishing stricter null models and analyzing the mechanism behind performance gains in constrained topologies.
topics.approach: We develop a method to interpret performance advantages in connectome-constrained neural networks by comparing them against degree-preserving rewired null graphs and naive self-loop-matched random controls. The approach will utilize a flyvis-based study framework on the Drosophila connectome dataset to analyze early loss, mean activity, and runtime metrics. We will also incorporate a weak control ensemble and pre-training activity-scale diagnostic to ensure that observed improvements are attributable to genuine topological constraints rather than initialization artifacts.

topics.title: Topological Rigidity of Complex and Quaternionic Moment-Angle Manifolds
topics.rationale: While equivariant topological rigidity has been established for some manifolds, the category of locally linear actions for complex and quaternionic moment-angle manifolds remains underexplored. This research addresses the gap by providing full equivariant rigidity results for quaternionic manifolds with four-dimensional quoric quotients and higher dimensions based on degree-4 characteristic classes. Understanding this rigidity is crucial for developing robust topological invariants in high-dimensional data analysis.
topics.approach: We investigate the equivariant topological rigidity of complex and quaternionic moment-angle manifolds by reducing the classification problem to the equivarant rigidity of quasitoric (or quoric) quotients. The method involves classifying associated principal bundles and locally linear actions, utilizing characteristic classes of degree-4 to establish the rigidity statement. This theoretical framework will be applied to analyze the structural stability of these manifolds under local linear deformations.

topics.title: Contraction and Hourglass Persistence for Learning on Graphs and Cells
topics.rationale: Existing Persistent Homology methods in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) rely on traversing an increasing sequence of subgraphs, which limits their expressivity, learnability, and stability. This research identifies specific limitations in the sequence of subgraphs used in PH methods and proposes a new contraction and hourglass persistence strategy to address these issues.
topics.approach: We introduce a contraction and hourglass persistence framework designed specifically for learning on graphs, simplices, and cells. This approach will modify the standard PH pipeline by incorporating contraction sequences and hourglass filters to improve the stability of learned topological features. We will evaluate this method on standard real-world graph datasets to assess improvements in expressivity, learnability, and stability compared to traditional increasing subgraph traversals.

topics.title: Identifying Non-Isomorphic Graph Families via Motif-Based Persistence with Covariance Nulling
topics.rationale: Existing graph isomorphism methods struggle with symmetries and lack the ability to perfectly distinguish non-isomorphic graphs in demanding families. The provided literature highlights gaps in balancing accuracy with computational cost, specifically in motif-based filtrations where existing methods often fail to account for data covariance structures that mimic subtype claims. This topic addresses the critical need for a framework that leverages persistent homology to detect topological differences while nullifying false positives caused by data covariance, which is a significant gap in current validation approaches for topological data analysis. By integrating null models specifically designed to account for covariance artifacts, this research offers a robust solution for distinguishing genuine subtypes in complex graph families. The proposed approach combines persistent homology with degree-based filtrations and density-based approaches to ensure sensitivity and topological performance. This work is timely as it bridges the gap between theoretical graph isomorphism and practical property prediction in real-world data, addressing the limitations of existing edge-weighting and curvature-based methods.
topics.approach: We will construct a novel motif-based filtration framework that incorporates a Gaussian null model to simulate data covariance structures. The method will utilize persistent homology to extract topological features from the graph, specifically focusing on cycle-density filtrations and degree-based filtrations. We will implement a test statistic comparing the mean-level differentiation between Mapper communities against the null model predictions to distinguish genuine subtypes from covariance artifacts. The study will be conducted using real-world data from molecular chemistry and social networks, applying cycle-density and density-based filtrations to ensure high sensitivity. Computational efficiency will be optimized through edge-weighting schemes tailored to the specific graph families studied.

topics.title: Efficient and Accurate Graph Isomorphism via Topological Data Analysis and Covariance Nulling
topics.rationale: Current graph isomorphism methods often rely on heuristics that fail to capture subtle topological differences or struggle with large-scale graphs. The literature emphasizes the need for methods that combine topological invariants with rigorous statistical testing to avoid false positives. This research addresses the gap in integrating persistent homology with covariance-nulling techniques to ensure that detected subtypes are statistically significant and not artifacts of data structure. By leveraging the properties of persistent homology and degree-based filtrations, the proposed method offers a robust alternative to existing edge-weighting and curvature-based approaches. The study will focus on high-dimensional graph families where traditional methods fail, ensuring both high sensitivity and topological accuracy. The integration of null models specifically designed to account for data covariance provides a critical advancement over existing validation frameworks, making this work highly relevant for real-world applications in network science and chemistry. The proposed approach combines persistent homology with degree-based filtrations and density-based approaches to ensure high sensitivity and topological performance, addressing the limitations of existing edge-weighting and curvature-based methods.

topics.title: Cross-Backend Compatibility of Quantum Neural Networks via Unified Computational Graphs
topics.rationale: Recent advancements in framework-agnostic architectures have highlighted the fragmentation of quantum machine learning (QML) software, where models developed in TensorFlow Quantum often fail to execute on PennyLane backends or vice versa. This creates a significant bottleneck for scientific discovery, as researchers cannot easily port models between hardware providers. By leveraging the three pluggable data encoding strategies (amplitude, angle, and instantaneous quantum polynomial encoding) proposed in recent literature, this research can establish a robust method for translating quantum circuits across diverse hardware platforms like IBM Quantum, Amazon Braket, and Azure Quantum. The integration with classical co-processors such as JAX and PyTorch further enhances portability, addressing the need for a unified computational graph that ensures training time parity with native implementations.
topics.approach: This study will implement a unified computational graph architecture that abstracts the hardware-specific execution layers, allowing for seamless translation of quantum circuits between TensorFlow, PyTorch, and JAX. We will utilize the three proposed data encoding strategies to map quantum states to classical representations, ensuring compatibility with existing classical backends. The approach involves constructing a hardware abstraction layer (HAL) that interfaces with multiple backends simultaneously, while exporting models to ONNX format for standardization. Validation will be conducted by training and testing QNNs on the Iris, Wine, and MNIST-4 datasets across different hardware providers to measure overhead and accuracy.

topics.title: Magnetically Insensitive Qubit States for Enhanced Coherence in Trapped Ions
topics.rationale: Current trapped ion quantum computers are limited by the sensitivity of qubits to external magnetic fields, particularly on field-sensitive Zeeman levels of valence electrons like in 40Ca+ and 88Sr+. This sensitivity restricts qubit coherence times (T2*) and limits the range of experiments that can be performed without active magnetic shielding. The recent synthesis of magnetically insensitive qubit states in metastable electronic D-states offers a promising solution to this challenge. By utilizing the experimental synthesis and theoretical modeling of these states, we can achieve coherent operations and flopping without the decoherence caused by magnetic field fluctuations. This work will provide a theoretical framework and experimental validation to demonstrate the feasibility of long-coherence qubits, which is critical for scaling up quantum algorithms.
topics.approach: We will employ a combined experimental and theoretical approach to synthesize and characterize magnetically insensitive qubit states in metastable electronic D-states of trapped ions such as 138Ba+. The methodology involves precise laser cooling and state preparation to isolate the D-state, followed by coherent operations to verify the insensitivity to magnetic fields. We will measure qubit coherence times (T2*) under varying magnetic field conditions to quantify the improvement over standard Zeeman levels. The results will be compared against existing benchmarks to assess the impact on quantum algorithm performance and potential applications in quantum simulation.

topics.title: Digital Twin Frameworks for Neutral-Atom Quantum Processors and Protocol Development
topics.rationale: Developing and simulating quantum protocols, hardware configurations, and digital twins for neutral-atom quantum processors remains a significant hurdle due to the complexity of modeling atoms, optical tweezers, laser fields, and interactions. Existing tools often require manual definition of Hamiltonians, which is error-prone and time-consuming. AtomTwin.jl represents a promising solution by modeling these components natively from physical geometry and parameters, offering a hardware-level instruction sequence and high-performance solvers for coupled quantum and classical dynamics. This gap in automated simulation tools hinders the rapid iteration required in modern quantum computing research, necessitating a robust framework that integrates physical parameters directly into the simulation model.
topics.approach: This research will develop and benchmark AtomTwin.jl, a physics-native digital twin framework that automatically models atoms, optical tweezers, laser fields, and interactions based on input physical geometry and parameters. We will implement high-performance solvers for coupled quantum and classical dynamics to simulate realistic noise processes and hardware imperfections. The framework will be extended to accommodate various atomic species and hardware configurations, allowing for the creation of digital twins that can predict protocol performance before experimental execution. Performance benchmarks will be generated using Ytterbium-171 atoms in moveable tweezers to evaluate the accuracy and efficiency of the simulation.

topics.title: Accelerating Quantum Many-Body Algorithm Development via LLM-Assisted Translation
topics.rationale: Translating quantum many-body theory into scalable software traditionally requires months of effort, creating a significant barrier to the adoption of new algorithms in the quantum field. Existing workflows lack automated mechanisms to bridge the gap between abstract theoretical formulations and executable code, leading to inefficiencies in algorithm development and deployment. Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) offer the potential to accelerate this translation process, potentially enabling zero-shot generation of tensor network algorithms from natural language descriptions of physical systems. Integrating LLM-assisted workflows into the quantum development pipeline could significantly reduce the time-to-market for new quantum algorithms and improve the reproducibility of research results.
topics.approach: This study will design and implement a multi-stage LLM-assisted workflow that translates quantum many-body theory into scalable software. The workflow will involve using LLMs to parse theoretical descriptions of physical systems and generate candidate tensor network algorithms, followed by automated validation and refinement steps to ensure correctness. We will analyze the performance of this workflow against traditional manual development methods to quantify the acceleration in development cycles. The approach will focus on identifying suboptimal aspects of current generation pipelines and proposing improvements to enhance the accuracy and efficiency of the translation process.

topics.title: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Optimization for Metastable Ion Trap Qubits Using D-State Encoding
topics.rationale: Recent advancements in ion trap architectures, specifically the synthesis of magnetically insensitive qubits in metastable D_3/2 states, have opened new avenues for photonic interfacing and fault tolerance. However, existing literature primarily focuses on coherent operations within these states rather than the hybrid optimization strategies required to stabilize metastable states against decoherence. Furthermore, there is a lack of systematic benchmarks comparing classical gradient-based methods against quantum-native approaches for optimizing qubit initialization and readout protocols in this specific metastable manifold. Addressing this gap is critical for scaling trapped ion systems beyond current coherence limits.
topics.approach: This research will employ a hybrid optimization framework combining the D_3/2 state encoding from [2] with a classical variational quantum eigensolver (VQE) adapted for the specific energy landscape of the 138Ba+ system. The approach will utilize the theoretical coherence times derived in [2] to design experimentally feasible pulse sequences, while leveraging the framework-agnostic principles from [1] to ensure hardware abstraction compatibility across different ion trap platforms. Data will be generated through simulation of the 138Ba+ atomic system dynamics, validated against experimental datasets from [2].
datasets: Ion trap coherence time T2* measurements

topics.title: ArrowFlow-Enhanced Ordinal Classification in Neutral-Atom Quantum Processors
topics.rationale: Neutral-atom quantum processors offer high-fidelity control over individual atoms, yet current deep learning paradigms struggle with the inherent discrete nature of quantum states, particularly in permutation-based architectures. The gap between continuous floating-point optimization and the integer-only, permutation-based computation of ArrowFlow remains a significant hurdle for practical deployment in hardware-constrained environments. By integrating the permutation-based ranking filters of ArrowFlow with the native digital twin capabilities of AtomTwin.jl, we can bridge the gap between theoretical quantum mechanics and practical machine learning. This research will evaluate the accuracy and privacy cost of ordinal representation learning in neutral-atom systems compared to standard gradient-based methods.
topics.approach: The proposed method involves training a neural network to operate entirely within the ArrowFlow architecture, utilizing permutation-matrix accumulation and Spearman's footrule distance to learn ordinal representations. We will construct a digital twin of Ytterbium-171 atoms in moveable tweezers using the AtomTwin framework to simulate the quantum dynamics required for the training process. The dataset will include preference data and UCI tabular benchmarks, with metrics focusing on clean accuracy and privacy cost.

topics.title: TurboQuant Variants for High-Fidelity Quantum Error Correction in Superconducting Circuits
topics.rationale: TurboQuant represents a significant evolution in quantum error correction, yet recent analysis suggests that its current scalar scale parameters ($S=1$) and residual quantization methods may introduce suboptimal accuracy compared to the unbiased EDEN scheme. Despite the theoretical promise of TurboQuant, there is a lack of empirical validation on how these specific parameter choices impact the fidelity of quantum operations in superconducting qubit systems. Bridging this gap requires a rigorous benchmarking study that compares TurboQuant variants against the EDEN and DRIVE schemes under realistic noise conditions.
topics.approach: We will conduct a systematic evaluation of TurboQuant$_{mse}$ and TurboQuant$_{prod}$ variants using a custom dataset of superconducting qubit noise profiles. The approach will involve parameter sweeps to identify optimal residual quantization methods and scalar scales that maximize the fidelity of quantum operations. By comparing these methods against the unbiased EDEN scheme, we can isolate the specific contributions of TurboQuant's design choices to its performance degradation.

topics.title: Out-of-Air Computation Framework for Structured Extraction from Wireless Superposition Channels
topics.rationale: Traditional Out-of-Air Computation (AirComp) relies on pre-embedding computation into transmitted waveforms, which often requires ideal wireless conditions or massive antenna arrays. The proposed Out-of-Air Computation framework addresses these limitations by extracting computation directly from the wireless superposition, enabling structured extraction even in noisy, non-ideal MAC environments. This shift is crucial for enabling distributed quantum sensing and secure communication in environments where pre-embedding is impractical or impossible.
topics.approach: The research will implement a joint source-channel coding scheme that decouples resolution and power constraints, utilizing a multi-layer nested lattice architecture to process data continuously. We will simulate the wireless superposition channel dynamics to validate the continuous-valued device data processing capabilities of the framework. The method will be tested on structured coding scenarios to demonstrate the feasibility of extraction without pre-embedding.
datasets: Multi-layer nested lattice architecture test cases

topics.title: LLM-Assisted Acceleration of Quantum Many-Body Algorithm Development via Paper-to-Program Translation
topics.rationale: Translating complex quantum many-body theory into scalable software has traditionally been a bottleneck, requiring months of manual effort to implement tensor network algorithms. While recent LLM-assisted workflows show promise, there is a critical need for a multi-stage, automated pipeline that can reliably generate high-fidelity tensor network algorithms directly from theoretical papers without human intervention. Integrating these capabilities with the rigorous validation methods used in quantum error correction could significantly accelerate the pace of quantum computing research.
topics.approach: This project will develop a multi-stage workflow using Large Language Models to parse quantum many-body papers and generate candidate tensor network algorithms. The generated code will undergo rigorous benchmarking against established quantum algorithms to ensure accuracy and scalability. We will evaluate the efficiency of this translation process compared to traditional manual implementation methods.
datasets: System Dynamics AI assistance task datasets

articles_with_facts.title: ArrowFlow: Hierarchical Machine Learning in the Space of Permutations
articles_with_facts.abstract: We introduce ArrowFlow, a machine learning architecture that operates entirely in the space of permutations. Its computational units are ranking filters, learned orderings that compare inputs via Spearman's footrule distance and update through permutation-matrix accumulation, a non-gradient rule rooted in displacement evidence. articles_with_facts.title: Neuromorphic Computing for Low-Power Artificial Intelligence
articles_with_facts.abstract: Classical computing is beginning to encounter fundamental limits of energy efficiency. This presents a challenge that can no longer be solved by strategies such as increasing circuit density or refining standard semiconductor processes. The growing computational and memory demands of artificial intelligence (AI) require disruptive innovation in how information is represented, stored, communicated, and processed. articles_with_facts.title: Out-of-Air Computation: Enabling Structured Extraction from Wireless Superposition
articles_with_facts.abstract: Over-the-air computation (AirComp) has traditionally been built on the principle of pre-embedding computation into transmitted waveforms or on exploiting massive antenna arrays, often requiring the wireless multiple-access channel (MAC) to operate under conditions that approximate an ideal computational medium. articles_with_facts.title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Maximizing Quantum Fisher Information in Time-Dependent Many-Body Systems
articles_with_facts.abstract: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) sets the ultimate precision limit for parameter estimation and is therefore a central quantity in quantum metrology. In time-dependent many-body systems, however, maximizing QFI is a highly non-trivial task due to the combined effects of non-commutativity, control complexity, and the exponential growth of the Hilbert space. articles_with_facts.title: InCoder-32B-Thinking: Industrial Code World Model for Thinking
articles_with_facts.abstract: Industrial software development across chip design, GPU optimization, and embedded systems lacks expert reasoning traces showing how engineers reason about hardware constraints and timing semantics. In this work, we propose InCoder-32B-Thinking, trained on the data from the Error-driven Chain-of-Thought (ECoT) synthesis framework with an industrial code world model (ICWM) to generate reasoning traces. articles_with_facts.abstract: In this paper, we apply the deterministic quantum imaginary time evolution (QITE) algorithm to obtain the ground state of a two-dimensional pure $\mathbb{Z}_2$ lattice gauge theory. articles_with_facts.abstract: We present a systematic evaluation of large language model families -- spanning both proprietary cloud APIs and locally-hosted open-source models -- on two purpose-built benchmarks for System Dynamics AI assistance: the \textbf{CLD Leaderboard} (53 tests, structured causal loop diagram extraction) and the \textbf{Discussion Leaderboard} (interactive model discussion, feedback explanation, and model building coaching). articles_with_facts.title: Hybrid Fourier Neural Operator for Surrogate Modeling of Laser Processing with a Quantum-Circuit Mixer
articles_with_facts.abstract: Data-driven surrogates can replace expensive multiphysics solvers for parametric PDEs, yet building compact, accurate neural operators for three-dimensional problems remains challenging: in Fourier Neural Operators, dense mode-wise spectral channel mixing scales linearly with the number of retained Fourier modes, inflating parameter counts and limiting real-time deployability. topics.title: Cross-Ecosystem Quantum Neural Network Export: Bridging Vendor Lock-In via Unified Graphs and Hardware Abstraction Layers
topics.rationale: Recent advances in framework-agnostic quantum neural networks (QNNs) highlight the critical need to abstract away vendor-specific interfaces to prevent fragmentation between TensorFlow Quantum, PyTorch, JAX, and cloud backends like IBM Quantum and Amazon Braket. The proposed approach leverages the multi-framework export pipeline and ONNX metadata from [1] to create a unified computational graph, enabling transparent access to diverse backends while maintaining hardware abstraction layer (HAL) compatibility. This addresses the identified gap where existing QML systems suffer from severe vendor lock-in, hindering scientific discovery by isolating researchers behind specific ecosystem boundaries.
topics.approach: We propose a hybrid architecture that integrates three pluggable data encoding strategies (amplitude, angle, and instantaneous quantum polynomial encoding) with a hardware abstraction layer. The method involves constructing a unified computational graph that exports intermediate states to ONNX format, allowing classical co-processors to run simultaneously. We will implement a multi-framework export pipeline that dynamically selects the optimal encoding strategy based on the target hardware's capabilities, ensuring training time parity within 8% overhead across different quantum simulators and backends.

topics.title: Cognitive Quantization-Aware Memory Systems for Zero-LLM Agents: Overcoming the Forgetting Paradox in AI Coding Assistants
topics.rationale: AI coding agents face a paradox where they possess vast parametric knowledge yet cannot retain conversations from hours ago due to single-channel vector retrieval and cloud-dependent operations. The proposed research utilizes the Zero-LLM Agent Memory Systems from [2] to implement Fisher-Rao Quantization-Aware Distance (FRQAD) and Ebbinghaus Adaptive Forgetting mechanisms. By integrating cognitive retrieval with lifecycle-aware quantization, we aim to address the gap between static vector databases and the dynamic, multi-hop recall required for effective agent behavior, specifically improving discriminative power in adversarial scenarios.
topics.approach: The study will focus on developing a 7-channel cognitive retrieval architecture that combines long-term implicit memory via soft prompts with FRQAD-based distance metrics. We will evaluate the system's ability to maintain mode-specific recall (Mode A, C) and measure the impact of quantization levels on adversarial robustness. The proposed framework will be tested against standard benchmarking tools to assess the trade-off between memory capacity and retrieval accuracy.
